Your PC will run hot during intense gaming sessions, so you’ll need to optimize the airflow to avoid overheating your components. While installing new fans is one way you can improve the overall temperature of your gaming rig, managing the number of intake and exhaust fans is just as important.

The same is true for the overall fan layout and the type of air pressure inside your PC case. There are a ton of factors that can affect the airflow, and we'll walk you through everything you need to know to get better cooling performance out of your PC.

Related
How to make your PC quieter
Your gaming PC can generate a lot of noise, and we're here to help you silence it!

How to choose the case fans

When picking fans for your cabinet, you should always consider their RPM, CFM, noise levels, and size.

The RPM, or rotations per minute, is a measure of the speed at which the fan blades spin. Most case fans run at 500-2000 RPM, and increasing the RPM can enhance their cooling capacity at the expense of increasing noise levels and reducing their longevity.

The CFM (Cubic Feet per Minute) of your fan refers to the volume of air it can push through your cabinet. Depending on their CFM, case fans are divided into static pressure and airflow fans. Static pressure fans are built to push a large volume of air through obstructions and narrow gaps, making them the perfect choice for radiators and cases which lack air inlets in their front panels. In contrast, airflow fans are better at pushing air when there aren’t any obstructions in their path. If you have a mesh front panel and decent cable management that doesn't disrupt the airflow, you should really go with airflow fans.

Noise level, as the name suggests, is the amount of noise generated by the fans in decibels (dB). Silent case fans produce barely audible noise in the 15 dB range, while performance fans can hit as high as 30-40 dB. With most fans, increasing the RPM will have a considerable impact on the noise level: the faster it spins, the more noise it creates, though there are some premium fans out there that are relatively quiet even under heavy RPM.

Finally, case fans come in many sizes, with 120mm and 140mm being the most common. Assuming they have the same RPM, you should always use 140mm (and larger) fans as intakes instead of smaller 120mm. The reason for this is that two 140mm fans can push the same amount of air as three 120mm fans, at much lower RPM and noise levels.

Intake vs exhaust fans

Simply put, an intake fan blows cool air into the cabinet while an exhaust fan pushes warm air out of it. All fans have intake and exhaust sides, and their orientation determines how they direct the airflow inside your case. You'll need a combination of both intake and exhaust fans to maintain the proper air pressure (more on that later) inside your PC.

How to determine the fan orientation

Cabinet fans have one or two arrows on the side that you can use to find their intake (air in) and exhaust (air out) sides. The first arrow will point in the direction of the airflow, and the second one will show which direction the fan blades rotate.

If your fans don’t have these arrows, you can use the structure of the fan to determine its orientation instead.

The exhaust side has an X-shaped spoke that holds the fans in place. This side is easy to identify as you’ll immediately notice the fan cables coming out of it. The intake side of the fan will have the fans in a convex orientation, i.e., the fans will be curving away from you and there won't be any X-shaped bars on that side.

Optimizing the PC for airflow

The front panel of your cabinet has a noticeable impact on air pressure. Cases with mesh front panels almost always have lower temperatures because the perforated mesh surface provides better airflow than glass or solid front panels. Unfortunately, this also means that mesh front panels attract more dust, but you should be fine as long as your cabinet has decent dust filters. You can even forgo all the side panels and go for an open-air case if you don't mind cleaning it out regularly.

When considering dust filters, keep in mind that they not only block dirt and grime from getting into your computer, but they also create obstructions for the air that flows into the case. Of course, this doesn’t mean you should discard all dust filters, but you should definitely keep them clean (to avoid further air blockage), and it may even be worth testing your PC without them if the system temperatures start to hit the dangerous zones even after setting up all the fans properly.

Another factor in fan configuration can be the case itself. The number of fans that you can slot into your gaming rig depends on the size of the cabinet, with some full tower cases supporting as many as ten fans.

Large cabinets also include mounting areas for AIO coolers and custom liquid cooling blocks, which makes them more useful than small cases when it comes to supporting more cooling solutions. But that doesn't mean you should write off small cases altogether. Many premium mini-towers and small form-factor cases are designed to optimize airflow to ensure your system operates at better temperatures.

Positive vs negative vs neutral air pressure

The next step is to decide on the type of air pressure you want in the case. Each has its pros and cons, and it's wise to test out the different air pressure configurations to find out the best one for your PC.

  1. Positive air pressure (In > Out): In this setup, the amount of cool air flowing in through the intake fans is greater than the hot air expelled by the exhaust fans. This type of air pressure provides decent cooling and is highly effective at lowering the amount of dust buildup inside your PC, as most cases are equipped with dust filters around the intake fans. However, you should avoid excess positive air pressure because it can result in a lot of turbulence and prevent the cooler air from reaching the thermally challenged parts of your PC.
  2. Negative air pressure (Out > In): As the name suggests, this is the exact opposite setup, where the exhaust fans pull more air out than the intake fans bring in, so that they can remove heat at a much faster rate. Assuming you keep your PC in a well-ventilated room, this setup can (theoretically) lead to better temperatures than positive airflow. Unfortunately, negative air pressure causes significant dust buildup inside, because air can flow in from all the holes and gaps in your chassis, including the openings that aren't covered with dust filters.
  3. Neutral air pressure (In = Out): With this setup, the amount of air entering via the intake fans is equal to the amount of hot air blowing out of the exhaust fans. If you want a balanced cooling setup that balances adequate cooling and lower dust buildup, a neutral air pressure configuration is your best bet. Sadly, it's difficult to maintain perfectly neutral air pressure, so most users should try to aim for slightly positive air pressure.

Where to place the case fans

For optimal air pressure, you should aim for more intake fans than exhaust fans. The air blasted into the cabinet from the intakes should have a clear path to the exhaust, otherwise, you'll have a lower cooling efficiency due to air turbulence.

The front panel of the case should have intake fans that will directly blow air towards the RAM, CPU, and GPU, while the fan slot adjacent to the I/O ports at the back of the case should only have exhaust fans. For most mid-sized towers, you should aim for two 140mm fans around the intake, though you can go for three 120mm fans if you don't mind higher noise levels. The exhaust can be a 120mm fan, as most cases don't support larger-sized fans around the exhaust. Here are some great fan layouts for a typical mid-tower cabinet:

  1. 5-fan setup: For an ideal 5-fan configuration, you'd want to place three fans at the front panel to blast a lot of cool air into the case. Next, attach an exhaust fan at the back of the case and another one in the top left corner (right above the CPU) to remove the hot air from your system. Out of all the non-liquid cooler setups, this is the ideal one to pick because it provides adequate cooling for every component of your PC.
  2. 4-fan setup: If you lack enough case fans for a 5-fan setup, you can go for a neutral airflow setup with two intake fans at the front panel, one exhaust fan at the rear end, and another exhaust at the top. Alternatively, you could remove the exhaust fan from the top of the case, and install it at the front panel as an intake. If you go with this adapted layout, you'll need to reduce the RPM of the intakes or you'll end up with excess positive air pressure.
  3. 3-fan setup: I'd advise against going lower than four fans if you're using a mid-tower case, but you can go with two intakes at the front panel, with an exhaust at the back if you want. Your system won't win any awards for proper cooling, but you shouldn't see abnormally high temperatures with this setup.

Now, you might have realized that even with the 5-fan configuration, we've left a fan slot empty at the top of the case. While you can install an exhaust there, I recommend leaving it empty because it can cause unnecessary air turbulence inside the case, and the extra space is better suited for adding a radiator instead of a fan.

If you have an AIO liquid cooler, you can either mount the radiator along the front panel, with the fans acting as intakes, or install the radiator at the top and have the fans run as exhausts. If you have some extra space inside your case, you can place multiple fans on either side of the radiator to maximize the amount of air flowing from the front panel. Otherwise, mounting the radiator at the top of the cabinet is also a valid option.

Make sure the tubing side lies at the bottom if you install the radiator at the front panel. In most cabinets, mounting the radiator with the tubing side at the top causes the pump to be placed higher than the tubes, which results in the air from the radiator getting sucked into the pump. This can damage the liquid cooler in the long run. If it's not possible to have the tubes at the bottom, you should install the radiator at the top of case or, at the very least, make sure the pump lies below the top of the radiator.

How to check the air pressure inside your case

A simple way to check the air pressure is to bring a thin piece of paper or tissue near small openings of your cabinet, like the PCIe slots. If the tissue is attracted toward the case, you have negative air pressure. If it gets blown away, there's a lot of positive air pressure inside the case.

You could also use an incense stick or a smoke machine to determine the air pressure, but I suggest sticking to the external tissue method, because the other two smoke methods could damage the internals. As mentioned earlier, it's best to maintain slightly positive air pressure, and the simplest way to achieve this is by running your exhaust fans at full speed and carefully lowering the RPM of the intake fans so that the paper gets slightly pushed away from the case.

Your PC should be able to breathe now

It's a bit tedious to configure the cabinet fans, but the improved system temperatures you will achieve will be worth all the hassle. Other miscellaneous factors can influence the airflow in your PC, including the amount of dust stuck to the fans and filters, as well as the cable management (or the lack thereof) inside your PC tower. Once you experiment with different fan layouts and air pressure configurations, you will find what works best for your system.

item.title: I turned my old PC into a Proxmox-powered home lab here's how it went
item.itunes:title: I turned my old PC into a Proxmox-powered home lab here's how it went
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Key Takeaways

  • Proxmox is ideal for running multiple projects due to its lightweight containers and efficient performance on older hardware.
  • Setting up a Proxmox server involves creating a bootable USB drive, modifying BIOS settings, and setting up the web UI.
  • The user-friendly Proxmox web UI allows for easy installation of virtual machines and containers, making it a great choice for repurposing old PCs.

From building a NAS to creating a self-hosted web server, there’s a lot you can do with a PC once it starts to show its age. But if you're a fan of trying out different projects, you can even convert an obsolete rig into a home lab that's capable of running different virtual machines and containers for all your computing needs.

After hearing good things about Proxmox, I decided to build a home lab with it to stop my secondary PC from gathering dust, and here’s everything I learned from the experiment.

Related
Breathe some new life into your old PC with these 5 operating systems
Not sure what to do with your ancient PC? You can restore its vitality with the help of these operating systems.

Why I installed Proxmox instead of using a simple hypervisor

General-purpose Type-2 hypervisors like VirtualBox are good enough to run a VM or two for lightweight tasks. However, since I’m planning to run NextCloud, a web server, and a host of other projects, there's no doubt that my Ryzen 5 1600-powered rig will buckle under pressure and turn into a stuttering mess should I try to run more than a few GUI operating systems on VirtualBox.

That’s where Proxmox came in handy: by running my virtual machines directly on the underlying hardware, I can cut down on the performance overhead. Heck, I don’t even need to create full-fledged virtual machines: since Proxmox supports lightweight Linux Containers, I could lower the resource consumption and increase the system’s efficiency when running multiple projects at the same time.

Creating the Proxmox server was simple...

If anything, the process is second nature for distro hoppers

Just like installing a new operating system, the first step in turning my faithful Ryzen 5 1600-powered dinosaur into a Proxmox server involved creating a bootable USB drive. As always, I used Rufus for this process, and within a few minutes, the flashing tool had finished writing the OS files on my flash drive.

Next, I had to modify the BIOS of my MSI B450 Gaming Plus Max motherboard to force the PC to boot from the newly configured USB drive. This was the simplest step in the entire procedure, as all I had to do was mash the Delete key as the BIOS booted up and change the boot priority of my PC from Crucial P3 500GB SSD to the 32GB USB drive containing the Proxmox installation files.

With that, I rebooted the system and used the Graphical installer to set up Proxmox on my SSD. While the installation was relatively quick, entering the web UI was far from simple, and I had to spend the next hour troubleshooting my setup.

... Accessing the web UI, was not

Keeping the Wi-Fi adapter plugged into my PC was a mistake

Some background info: Since the B450 motherboard I’d used lacked a built-in Wi-Fi module, I had to buy an external adapter from TP-Link for the times when I couldn’t use Ethernet.

Turns out, the adapter was conflicting with the LAN interface, which is why I kept running into the “This network can’t be reached” error message when I tried to open the web UI on Chrome. I finally realized what went wrong after checking the network devices with the ip -a command and failing to ping the Google.com and 1.1.1.1 servers. Luckily, unplugging the Wi-Fi adapter did the trick, and I was able to arrive at the login page of the web UI after my third attempt at installing Proxmox.

The Web UI is robust and laden with features

Better yet, it's nowhere near as complex as the TrueNAS interface

After typing root as the username and entering the same password I chose for the Proxmox server during its installation, the Proxmox web UI finally booted up. Before dabbling with LXC and KVM containers, I wanted to install a virtual machine for Q4OS on the Proxmox server. There were a handful of ways for me to get the ISO image, but I wanted to download it directly on my old PC instead of transferring it from my daily driver.

So, I went to the Storage Pools tab and used the Download from URL option to grab a Q4OS ISO. Creating the VM was a cinch, and within a few minutes, my lightweight Q4OS virtual machine was up and running. I repeated the procedure with Puppy Linux and Endeavour OS, and soon, I had three GUI-based operating systems running on my aged Ryzen 5 1600 processor.

To wrap things up, I decided to create a NextCloud container. To further simplify things, I decided to use the turnkey NextCloud container template. Since it wasn’t available by default, I headed to the Shell tab and used the pveam update command to add it to my Proxmox server. Once I’d downloaded the template, I created a container for the NextCloud server, and the process was so quick that going through the NextCloud initialization tool took longer than creating multiple VMs and a container!

I heartily recommend turning an old PC into a Proxmox server

Initially, I had doubts about the performance of the home server, especially after my Ryzen 5 1600's disappointing performance in newer games and virtualization tasks. However, it was surprising to see the seven-year-old budget processor perform so well in a home lab.

When I create a project on my PCs, laptops, microcontrollers, and SBCs, I continue to use them for a few weeks before wiping the storage to make space for new projects. But I was so impressed by my makeshift home lab that I might just buy a new SSD to keep Proxmox permanently installed on my PC!
item.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Proxmox is ideal for running multiple projects due to its lightweight containers and efficient performance on older hardware.
  • Setting up a Proxmox server involves creating a bootable USB drive, modifying BIOS settings, and setting up the web UI.
  • The user-friendly Proxmox web UI allows for easy installation of virtual machines and containers, making it a great choice for repurposing old PCs.

From building a NAS to creating a self-hosted web server, there’s a lot you can do with a PC once it starts to show its age. But if you're a fan of trying out different projects, you can even convert an obsolete rig into a home lab that's capable of running different virtual machines and containers for all your computing needs.

After hearing good things about Proxmox, I decided to build a home lab with it to stop my secondary PC from gathering dust, and here’s everything I learned from the experiment.

Related
Breathe some new life into your old PC with these 5 operating systems
Not sure what to do with your ancient PC? You can restore its vitality with the help of these operating systems.

Why I installed Proxmox instead of using a simple hypervisor

General-purpose Type-2 hypervisors like VirtualBox are good enough to run a VM or two for lightweight tasks. However, since I’m planning to run NextCloud, a web server, and a host of other projects, there's no doubt that my Ryzen 5 1600-powered rig will buckle under pressure and turn into a stuttering mess should I try to run more than a few GUI operating systems on VirtualBox.

That’s where Proxmox came in handy: by running my virtual machines directly on the underlying hardware, I can cut down on the performance overhead. Heck, I don’t even need to create full-fledged virtual machines: since Proxmox supports lightweight Linux Containers, I could lower the resource consumption and increase the system’s efficiency when running multiple projects at the same time.

Creating the Proxmox server was simple...

If anything, the process is second nature for distro hoppers

Just like installing a new operating system, the first step in turning my faithful Ryzen 5 1600-powered dinosaur into a Proxmox server involved creating a bootable USB drive. As always, I used Rufus for this process, and within a few minutes, the flashing tool had finished writing the OS files on my flash drive.

Next, I had to modify the BIOS of my MSI B450 Gaming Plus Max motherboard to force the PC to boot from the newly configured USB drive. This was the simplest step in the entire procedure, as all I had to do was mash the Delete key as the BIOS booted up and change the boot priority of my PC from Crucial P3 500GB SSD to the 32GB USB drive containing the Proxmox installation files.

With that, I rebooted the system and used the Graphical installer to set up Proxmox on my SSD. While the installation was relatively quick, entering the web UI was far from simple, and I had to spend the next hour troubleshooting my setup.

... Accessing the web UI, was not

Keeping the Wi-Fi adapter plugged into my PC was a mistake

Some background info: Since the B450 motherboard I’d used lacked a built-in Wi-Fi module, I had to buy an external adapter from TP-Link for the times when I couldn’t use Ethernet.

Turns out, the adapter was conflicting with the LAN interface, which is why I kept running into the “This network can’t be reached” error message when I tried to open the web UI on Chrome. I finally realized what went wrong after checking the network devices with the ip -a command and failing to ping the Google.com and 1.1.1.1 servers. Luckily, unplugging the Wi-Fi adapter did the trick, and I was able to arrive at the login page of the web UI after my third attempt at installing Proxmox.

The Web UI is robust and laden with features

Better yet, it's nowhere near as complex as the TrueNAS interface

After typing root as the username and entering the same password I chose for the Proxmox server during its installation, the Proxmox web UI finally booted up. Before dabbling with LXC and KVM containers, I wanted to install a virtual machine for Q4OS on the Proxmox server. There were a handful of ways for me to get the ISO image, but I wanted to download it directly on my old PC instead of transferring it from my daily driver.

So, I went to the Storage Pools tab and used the Download from URL option to grab a Q4OS ISO. Creating the VM was a cinch, and within a few minutes, my lightweight Q4OS virtual machine was up and running. I repeated the procedure with Puppy Linux and Endeavour OS, and soon, I had three GUI-based operating systems running on my aged Ryzen 5 1600 processor.

To wrap things up, I decided to create a NextCloud container. To further simplify things, I decided to use the turnkey NextCloud container template. Since it wasn’t available by default, I headed to the Shell tab and used the pveam update command to add it to my Proxmox server. Once I’d downloaded the template, I created a container for the NextCloud server, and the process was so quick that going through the NextCloud initialization tool took longer than creating multiple VMs and a container!

I heartily recommend turning an old PC into a Proxmox server

Initially, I had doubts about the performance of the home server, especially after my Ryzen 5 1600's disappointing performance in newer games and virtualization tasks. However, it was surprising to see the seven-year-old budget processor perform so well in a home lab.

When I create a project on my PCs, laptops, microcontrollers, and SBCs, I continue to use them for a few weeks before wiping the storage to make space for new projects. But I was so impressed by my makeshift home lab that I might just buy a new SSD to keep Proxmox permanently installed on my PC!

item.title: Best cases for Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra
item.itunes:title: Best cases for Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
We've seen more than a few smartphones releases in 2024, but the Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra remains a top pick despite being one of the first big releases of the year. This top-of-the-line phone in the Galaxy S lineup brings a bunch of small yet noteworthy improvements to the table, making it a compelling option to consider. From top-notch internals for solid performance to a versatile set of cameras and the ever-improving S-Pen stylus, it's all here in its best shape and form.

The only thing you need to know before buying this phone is that it's got a massive footprint. That's right, the Galaxy S24 Ultra is just as big and bulky as its predecessor, meaning you still may have a hard time using it if you're coming from a relatively smaller phone. You're going to need some good cases to protect your shiny new Galaxy S24 Ultra in case you end up dropping it. It also looks exactly like its predecessor, meaning it's not nearly as exciting as some other phones out there in terms of the design. Buying a case can fix that as well for you, as there are plenty of good-looking cases out there that not only protect your phone, but also change its look and feel.

  • Spigen Enzo Aramid Case for S24 Ultra
    Editor's choice

    The Spigen Enzo Aramid case is perfect for those who are looking for a simple case for their Galaxy S24 Ultra that adds a good amount of protection without adding too much bulk to it. It's made of Aramid and TPU materials to offer great durability, and it also as a carbon finish on the outside for sleek looks. The Spigen Enzo Aramid also has a soft suede interior to keep your phone's body from getting scratched, and it also has raised bezels around the display and the camera modules.

    $36 at Amazon
  • Bellroy leather case for Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra
    Premium pick

    The Bellroy leather case is a popular choice among those who prefer using a premium-looking case to protect their phones. It's available in three different colors — Black, Everglade, and Terracotta — all of which are made of eco-tanned leather and come with a microfiber lining. This particular case doesn't add too much bulk to the phone, either. Bellroy cases have precise cutouts, and they're also sturdier than most other leather cases out there, making your phone look and feel great in the hand.

    $55 at Amazon
  • Supcase Unicorn Beetle MAG XT Bumper Case for S24 Ultra
    Promoted Pick

    In partnership with Supcase

    This is another great option for S24 Ultra since it is crafted with shock-absorbing TPU and polycarbonate: both of which are known for their exceptional durability. There’s a zinc alloy camera cover for added protection to the camera lens too. Supcase claims that the camera cover hinge has been tested over 30,000 times, which gives the idea of quality. Best part? This cover is wireless charging compatible and comes with a 1-year warranty.

    $27 at SUPCASE $25 at Amazon
  • Ringke Fusion-X case for S24 Ultra
    Best value pick

    The Ringke Fusion-X case is perfect for those who are looking to buy a relatively affordable protective case for the Galaxy S24 Ultra. It comes with precise cutouts for all the ports and the camera lenses, and you also get a textured frame for added grip. It's also relatively slim for a military-grade shockproof case, and looks particularly great with a camo finish.

    $18 at Amazon
  • Ringke Fusion case for Galaxy S24 Ultra
    Simple clear case

    If you are looking for a simple, clear case for the Galaxy S24 Ultra then look no further than the Ringke Fusion clear case. This is one of the popular picks here at XDA, as it offers a good amount of protection for the phone while letting you show off the colors. You can also get a variant of this case that comes with a matte finish if you don't like how the glossy one feel in your hand.

    $14 at Amazon
  • Dbrand case for S24 Ultra
    Creative designs

    dbrand has also launched its range of S24 Ultra cases, each of which has a unique and appealing design. The case is MagSafe compatible and you also get clicky buttons for that tactile feel with each keypress. This particular case is a bit on the thicker side, but it offers impressive protection, along with a grippy frame on the sides to hold on to.

    $55 at dbrand
  • Samsung Standing Grip Case for Galaxy S24 Ultra
    Best silicone cover
    $38 $60 Save $22

    This soft touch silicone case made by Samsung is good for those who prefer simple-looking cases over something that's too rugged or bulky. The highlight of this particular case is that comes with a hand strap at the back that also doubles up as a kickstand for when you feel like setting up the phone on a flat surface for a hands-free media viewing experience.

    $38 at Amazon
  • i-Blason Cosmo Case for S24 Ultra
    Stylish design

    In partnership with i-Blason

    The i-Blason Cosmo case is perfect for those who love style and functionality. This slim, marble-designed case not only looks great but also offers solid protection. It comes with a built-in screen protector that supports fingerprint ID, and a unique camera cover that doubles as a kickstand. Plus, it's wireless charging friendly with strong magnets for easy charging. It's perfect for anyone who appreciates a blend of protection and elegance!

    $30 at Amazon
  • Poetic Guardian case for Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra
    360-degree protection

    The Poetic Guardian case for the Galaxy S24 Ultra stands out as a solid protective case as it offers complete 360-degree protection for the device. In addition to a protective shell for the back of the phone, you also get a built-in screen protector with this case. Notably, it also comes with a built-in camera stand, along with raised edges for added protection.

    $25 at Amazon
  • TORRAS Shockproof Designed for S24 Ultra
    Slim rugged case
    $18 $30 Save $12

    The TORRAS Guardian Series case combines style with top-tier protection to deliver a solid experience. It's one of the thinnest cases on the market, but it offers military-grade drop protection, complete with raised bezels and reinforced corners. It's ideal for those valuing a sleek, protective, and functional case.

    $18 at Amazon
  • OtterBox Defender case for Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra
    Superior drop protection
    $53 $65 Save $12

    If you are looking for the best rugged case for your phone with superior drop-protection, then consider buying this Defender case from OtterBox. It's available in a few different colors, all of which offer solid drop protection for your phone. Not only do you get raised edges for the display and the camera sensors at the back, but you also get port cover to secure the USB port. It also comes with a holster clip that doubles up as a kickstand.

    $53 at Amazon
  • CASEKOO Magnetic Ring Stand Case for S24 Ultra
    Magnetic metal ring stand

    If you want the maximum features in a case, but with minimal spending, this is it. The CASEKOO case features powerful MagSafe compatibility to support things like fast, efficient wireless charging. It also has a magnetic ring at the back that doubles as a kickstand for the phone. It's available in two colors, both of which have a matte translucent finish.

    $35 at Amazon

Which case is best for your Galaxy S24 Ultra?

There are plenty of Galaxy S24 Ultra cases out there, but I'd personally go with Spigen's Enzo Aramid Case as it looks great, offers a good amount of protection to the phone without making it any bulkier than it already is. The Bellroy leather case is also great if you are looking for something that's a bit more premium to compliment the premium price tag of the phone. Those looking for a more rugged case should consider buying either the Supcase UB MAG XT or the OtterBox Defender case. They both offer superior drop-protection, while bringing other meaningful improvements to the user experience.

Not all cases highlighted above come with a built-in screen protector, so be sure to grab one if you feel the need for that added display protection. And, while you are at it, be sure to check out the best chargers for Samsung Galaxy S24, which will work well for the Ultra as well.

item.description:
We've seen more than a few smartphones releases in 2024, but the Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra remains a top pick despite being one of the first big releases of the year. This top-of-the-line phone in the Galaxy S lineup brings a bunch of small yet noteworthy improvements to the table, making it a compelling option to consider. From top-notch internals for solid performance to a versatile set of cameras and the ever-improving S-Pen stylus, it's all here in its best shape and form.

The only thing you need to know before buying this phone is that it's got a massive footprint. That's right, the Galaxy S24 Ultra is just as big and bulky as its predecessor, meaning you still may have a hard time using it if you're coming from a relatively smaller phone. You're going to need some good cases to protect your shiny new Galaxy S24 Ultra in case you end up dropping it. It also looks exactly like its predecessor, meaning it's not nearly as exciting as some other phones out there in terms of the design. Buying a case can fix that as well for you, as there are plenty of good-looking cases out there that not only protect your phone, but also change its look and feel.

  • Spigen Enzo Aramid Case for S24 Ultra
    Editor's choice

    The Spigen Enzo Aramid case is perfect for those who are looking for a simple case for their Galaxy S24 Ultra that adds a good amount of protection without adding too much bulk to it. It's made of Aramid and TPU materials to offer great durability, and it also as a carbon finish on the outside for sleek looks. The Spigen Enzo Aramid also has a soft suede interior to keep your phone's body from getting scratched, and it also has raised bezels around the display and the camera modules.

    $36 at Amazon
  • Bellroy leather case for Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra
    Premium pick

    The Bellroy leather case is a popular choice among those who prefer using a premium-looking case to protect their phones. It's available in three different colors — Black, Everglade, and Terracotta — all of which are made of eco-tanned leather and come with a microfiber lining. This particular case doesn't add too much bulk to the phone, either. Bellroy cases have precise cutouts, and they're also sturdier than most other leather cases out there, making your phone look and feel great in the hand.

    $55 at Amazon
  • Supcase Unicorn Beetle MAG XT Bumper Case for S24 Ultra
    Promoted Pick

    In partnership with Supcase

    This is another great option for S24 Ultra since it is crafted with shock-absorbing TPU and polycarbonate: both of which are known for their exceptional durability. There’s a zinc alloy camera cover for added protection to the camera lens too. Supcase claims that the camera cover hinge has been tested over 30,000 times, which gives the idea of quality. Best part? This cover is wireless charging compatible and comes with a 1-year warranty.

    $27 at SUPCASE $25 at Amazon
  • Ringke Fusion-X case for S24 Ultra
    Best value pick

    The Ringke Fusion-X case is perfect for those who are looking to buy a relatively affordable protective case for the Galaxy S24 Ultra. It comes with precise cutouts for all the ports and the camera lenses, and you also get a textured frame for added grip. It's also relatively slim for a military-grade shockproof case, and looks particularly great with a camo finish.

    $18 at Amazon
  • Ringke Fusion case for Galaxy S24 Ultra
    Simple clear case

    If you are looking for a simple, clear case for the Galaxy S24 Ultra then look no further than the Ringke Fusion clear case. This is one of the popular picks here at XDA, as it offers a good amount of protection for the phone while letting you show off the colors. You can also get a variant of this case that comes with a matte finish if you don't like how the glossy one feel in your hand.

    $14 at Amazon
  • Dbrand case for S24 Ultra
    Creative designs

    dbrand has also launched its range of S24 Ultra cases, each of which has a unique and appealing design. The case is MagSafe compatible and you also get clicky buttons for that tactile feel with each keypress. This particular case is a bit on the thicker side, but it offers impressive protection, along with a grippy frame on the sides to hold on to.

    $55 at dbrand
  • Samsung Standing Grip Case for Galaxy S24 Ultra
    Best silicone cover
    $38 $60 Save $22

    This soft touch silicone case made by Samsung is good for those who prefer simple-looking cases over something that's too rugged or bulky. The highlight of this particular case is that comes with a hand strap at the back that also doubles up as a kickstand for when you feel like setting up the phone on a flat surface for a hands-free media viewing experience.

    $38 at Amazon
  • i-Blason Cosmo Case for S24 Ultra
    Stylish design

    In partnership with i-Blason

    The i-Blason Cosmo case is perfect for those who love style and functionality. This slim, marble-designed case not only looks great but also offers solid protection. It comes with a built-in screen protector that supports fingerprint ID, and a unique camera cover that doubles as a kickstand. Plus, it's wireless charging friendly with strong magnets for easy charging. It's perfect for anyone who appreciates a blend of protection and elegance!

    $30 at Amazon
